audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel chapter 12, first-pass draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Nathan's parable, property metaphors around women, direct confrontation, sexualized retaliation prophecy, confession without public repair, death of the child, grief, Bathsheba, Solomon/Jedidiah, Rabbah conquest, crown and spoil, and forced-labor/torture variants.

verses.restored: YHWH sent Nathan to David. He came and said, Two men were in one city, one rich and one poor.
Rich man had very many flocks and herds.

Poor man had nothing except one small ewe lamb he had bought. He sustained it, and it grew with him and his children. It ate from his portion, drank from his cup, lay in his bosom, and was like a daughter to him.
A traveler came to rich man. Rich man spared his own flock and herd and took poor man's ewe lamb, preparing it for traveler who came to him.
David's anger burned greatly against man. He said to Nathan, As YHWH lives, man who did this is a son of death.
He shall repay lamb fourfold, because he did this thing and because he had no compassion.
Nathan said to David, You are the man. Thus says YHWH, Divine of Israel: I anointed you king over Israel and rescued you from hand of Saul.
I gave you house of your lord and women of your lord into your household, and I gave you house of Israel and Judah. If this were too little, I would add more and more.
Why did you despise word of YHWH, doing evil in YHWH's eyes? Uriah the Hittite you struck with sword; his wife you took as your wife; him you killed by sword of children of Ammon.
Now sword shall not turn aside from your house, because you despised me and took wife of Uriah the Hittite to be your wife.
Thus says YHWH: Look, I am raising evil against you from your house. I will take your women before your eyes and give them to your neighbor, who will lie with your women before eyes of this sun.
You acted in secret, but this thing will be done before all Israel and before sun.
David said to Nathan, I have sinned against YHWH. Nathan said to David, YHWH also has passed over your sin; you shall not die.
Nevertheless, because by this deed you have utterly treated YHWH's enemies with contempt/caused contempt, the son born to you will surely die.
Nathan went to his house. YHWH struck child whom wife of Uriah bore to David, and he became gravely ill.
David sought Divine on behalf of child. David fasted, entered, and lay all night upon ground.
Elders of his house arose beside him to raise him from ground, but he was not willing and did not eat bread with them.

On seventh day child died. Servants of David feared to tell him child was dead, because they said, Look, while child lived we spoke to him and he did not listen. How then can we say child is dead? He may harm himself/do evil.
David saw his servants whispering and understood child was dead. David said to servants, Is child dead? They said, He is dead.
David arose from ground, washed, anointed himself, changed garments, came to house of YHWH, and bowed. He came to his house, asked, and they set bread before him, and he ate.
His servants said to him, What is this thing you have done? For living child you fasted and wept; when child died, you arose and ate bread.
He said, While child lived, I fasted and wept, because I said, Who knows? YHWH may be gracious to me and child may live.
Now he is dead. Why should I fast? Can I return him again? I am going to him, but he will not return to me.
David comforted Bathsheba his wife, came to her, and lay with her. She bore a son, and he called his name Solomon. YHWH loved him.
YHWH sent by hand of Nathan the prophet and called his name Jedidiah, for sake of YHWH.
Joab fought against Rabbah of children of Ammon and captured royal city.
Joab sent messengers to David and said, I fought against Rabbah and captured city of waters.
Now gather rest of people, camp against city, and capture it, lest I capture city and my name be called upon it.
David gathered all people, went to Rabbah, fought against it, and captured it.
He took crown of their king/Milcom from his head; its weight was a talent of gold with a precious stone. It was placed on David's head. He brought out spoil of city in very great abundance.

He brought out people who were in it and placed them under/at saws, iron picks, and iron axes, and made them pass through brickworks. Thus he did to all cities of children of Ammon. David and all people returned to Jerusalem.

translation_notes: Nathan confronts the king through a story that makes David recognize predatory taking. The parable's lamb is 'like a daughter'; Bathsheba herself is not Uriah's animal or property.
Verse 8 reflects ancient royal-household language that treats women as transferable. It must not be received as Divine endorsement of owning women.
Nathan names David as the killer of Uriah even though Ammonite weapons and Joab carried out the plan. Delegation does not dissolve command responsibility.

Verses 11-12 describe sexual violation of David's women as retaliation against David. Women may not be used as instruments of punishment, prophecy fulfillment, political messaging, or inherited consequences.
David's brief confession avoids his execution in the narrative, but forgiveness does not replace confession to survivors, truth-telling, reparations, loss of office, or institutional accountability.

The child's illness and death are morally intolerable as punishment for an adult's abuse. This text must never teach that children die because of parents' sexual sin or that infant death proves Divine anger.
Servants explicitly fear David may harm himself. His fasting and collapse require suicide-aware care rather than romantic spiritual heroism.
Bathsheba is again named as Uriah's wife in verse 15 and later as Bathsheba in verse 24. The birth of Solomon does not erase her abuse, Uriah, or the dead child.
Verse 31 is textually and translationally disputed between forced labor and torture. Neither reading makes conquest benign; both preserve coercion of Ammonite civilians.
safety_note: Maximum safeguards: Never use this chapter to blame a child for David's actions, reassure bereaved parents that death was punishment, or frame sexual violence against women as Divinely arranged justice. David's confession is not the end of accountability. Center Bathsheba, Uriah, the unnamed child, soldiers killed in the cover-up, and conquered Ammonite people.
